questions.q: Roughly how much cheaper is a Qwen Plus 50-msg/day workload than a Claude Opus 4.8 workload?
choices: About the same
About 2x cheaper
About 5x cheaper
About 20x cheaper
questions.explain: Qwen Plus runs ~$12-$25/mo at 50 msgs/day; Opus 4.8 runs ~$80-$150/mo. Roughly 5x cheaper on average.

questions.q: Per the source video, Qwen 3.7 Max's defining strength is:
choices: Cheapest output token price in the tier
Native multimodal understanding
Long-horizon agent workflow speed (hours of tool calls)
Best front-end design output
questions.explain: Qwen 3.7 Max is trained as an agent backbone - runs for hours and hundreds of tool calls without collapsing. Ancient Chinese building test: 8m 53s vs 2-3 hours on Kimi/MiniMax.

questions.q: What is the main gotcha with GLM 5.2?
choices: It refuses to write code
It is closed-weight with no API
It is too slow and bug-prone for live agentic work
It costs more than Claude Opus
questions.explain: GLM is 'too slow and bug-prone for live agentic game work.' Use it for one-shot UI and design; do not depend on it for production-critical pipelines.

questions.q: MiniMax M3's MSA architecture gives what advantage?
choices: Cheapest input token price in the tier
Best front-end design quality
~9.7x faster pre-fill and 15.6x faster decode at 1M tokens vs M2.7
Native video generation
questions.explain: MSA is the speed play behind MiniMax M3's long-context performance - 9.7x faster pre-fill and 15.6x faster decode at 1M tokens vs M2.7.

questions.q: When does the cost-efficient tier lose to frontier models?
choices: Always - cost-efficient means weaker
Never - cheap models are strictly better
On ambiguous, novel, or sustained multi-step reasoning tasks
Only when the user does not know what they are doing
questions.explain: Cost-efficient models win on well-defined tasks with verifiable success criteria. They lose on ambiguous prompts, novel creative work, and multi-hour sustained reasoning.
